notes: Monday May 18 — US markets are open as this brief is generated; today's closing tape is not yet confirmed. Per policy the most recent confirmed close (Friday May 15: $422.24) is shown, with a 0% placeholder change. Pre-market signals were modestly positive on the back of weekend Model Y price hikes (StockInvest's predicted fair open was $426.30, intraday range so far reported around $422-$435 with the bell open near $434), but no source has yet published a verified May 18 closing print. Tomorrow's brief will pick up Monday's official close.

news.sentiment_summary: Sentiment going into Monday's session is genuinely split. The bullish surprise is Tesla's first US Model Y price hike in two years ($500-$1,000 across trims), a tangible margin signal that the demand-on-discount era may be ending. The bearish overhang is unchanged: the Trump-Xi summit produced no China FSD approval and Wall Street's consensus 12-month target ($403.86) still sits below Friday's $422.24 close. Tesla China publicly denied that the US price hike will be replicated in China, capping the bull case. The narrative is being pulled in opposite directions by a real margin catalyst on one side and a colder China backdrop, rising long-end yields, and the freshly disclosed 17-crash robotaxi tally on the other — which is why the analyst-predicted open of just +0.96% looks rationally cautious despite the price-hike news.

items.headline: Tesla Raises US Model Y Prices by Up to $1,000 — First Hike in Two Years
items.summary: Over the weekend Tesla lifted Model Y Premium AWD and Premium RWD prices by $1,000 to $49,990 and $45,990 respectively, and raised the Model Y Performance AWD by $500 to $57,990. It is the first US price increase on Model Y in roughly two years and breaks a multi-year pattern of discounting to defend share. The market reads this as a confidence signal on demand and a direct positive for auto gross margin — the single biggest swing factor on every Q2 model.

items.headline: Tesla China Publicly Denies Model Y Price Hike Rumours in Domestic Market
items.summary: Hours after the US price increases were confirmed, Tesla China issued a statement explicitly denying that Model Y prices in mainland China will be raised. The denial caps the bull case: investors hoping the US move signalled a global pricing reset have to recalibrate. China remains a discount-driven, share-defending market where Tesla holds only ~3% NEV market share, and there is no margin tailwind coming from the world's largest EV market.

items.headline: Predicted Fair Open for Monday $426.30 — Analysts Position for Modest Bounce
items.summary: StockInvest's algorithmic forecast pegged TSLA's Monday May 18 fair open at $426.30 (+0.96% vs Friday's close), with an expected intraday band of $413.67-$430.81. The mild positive bias reflects the Model Y price-hike tailwind partially offsetting unresolved China FSD overhang and Treasury-yield pressure. Retail sentiment on Stocktwits cooled from 'extremely bullish' to 'bullish' over the weekend, mirroring the moderation.

items.headline: Wall Street 12-Month Target Still Below Spot After Six-Week Rally
items.summary: TipRanks' aggregation of 29 sell-side analysts puts Tesla's average 12-month target at $403.86 — about 4.4% below Friday's $422.24 close. Wedbush remains the high bull at $600 (Dan Ives) and GLJ the lone deep bear at $25.53. The mean target has been below spot for the first time in six weeks, meaning the average analyst now expects a negative one-year return from here. Last upgrade: UBS to $352 on April 14; Morgan Stanley sits at $425.

items.headline: Optimus V3 Lines Installing at Fremont; Cybercab Wash Site Breaks Ground in Vegas
items.summary: Tesla has begun installing first-generation Optimus humanoid production lines at Fremont, with the V3 reveal still flagged for late July or early August alongside Q2 earnings. Separately, ground broke on a dedicated Cybercab cleaning and servicing facility in Clark County, Nevada — small but real physical infrastructure for the seven-city H2 robotaxi rollout. Both are credible execution proof points but neither moves Q2 numbers.

analysis_7d.summary: Tesla's week followed a classic rally-and-reversal arc: a steady climb from around $398 to a Wednesday peak near $445 on China-summit optimism, then a single-day Friday give-back of most of those gains after the summit ended with no FSD approval. The long-term structure is undamaged — Golden Cross intact, price comfortably above the 200-day average — and Friday's drop happened on lighter-than-usual volume, which argues against panic selling. The short-term picture has nevertheless clearly cooled: price is below the 5-day average, RSI is flat at 44, MACD momentum is rolling over. The fresh wildcard arriving Monday is the weekend Model Y price hike, the first US price increase in two years; that is a real margin signal that could give the medium-term $416-$418 support zone a fighting chance to hold. Lose $416 and the path back to the high-$390s opens up; reclaim $432 and Wednesday's $445 resistance is back in view.

analysis_30d.summary: The 30-day picture remains a recovery story — Tesla is up 16.5% — but the cracks are getting more visible. The narrative drivers (Q1 earnings beat, robotaxi fleet quadrupling, FSD subscribers +51% YoY) are genuinely powerful, but each successive rally leg has met heavier resistance and Friday's reversal was the first clear signal that the easy money has been made. The stock is now boxed in a tight $416-$445 consolidation range, supported below by the 50-day moving average and capped above by last week's failed breakout zone. The weekend Model Y price hike is the first new bullish input in over a week — it directly addresses the auto gross margin question that Q2 will turn on — but it is partially neutralised by Tesla China refusing to follow suit and by Wall Street's mean target still sitting below spot. The trend is technically still 'up' but it has lost its swagger; until China FSD lands or Q2 numbers force a re-rate, expect choppier two-way action inside the current range.

outlook.recent_rating_change: UBS raised price target to $352 on April 14 (last formal upgrade); RBC reiterated Outperform on May 12 on 51% YoY FSD subscriber growth; Wedbush reiterated $600 high target on April 23; Morgan Stanley sits at $425

catalysts.detail: Street consensus sits at 390K-420K deliveries (a 9-17% sequential lift from Q1's 358K). Anything below 390K would confirm the slowdown thesis and likely re-test recent lows; anything above 420K would re-energise the bull case. The Model Y price hike will be tested directly here — did US demand stay sticky at the higher price?

EPS estimate $0.44 on $24.52B revenue. The Street wants auto gross margin back above 17% and visible early Cybercab revenue. The weekend US price hike makes that 17% margin bar materially easier to clear if order rates hold.

First-gen Optimus production lines are being installed at Fremont now, with V3 unveiling expected alongside Q2 earnings. A credible demo and a real production timeline would re-anchor the long-term AI thesis.

catalysts.timing: Q3 2026 (slipped from Q2 after May 15 summit)
catalysts.detail: Approval would let Tesla monetise FSD in the world's largest EV market and would reverse Friday's selloff overnight. Rejection — or further silence — is now largely priced in after the summit produced no deal.

catalysts.timing: Phoenix, Miami, Orlando, Tampa, Las Vegas — rolling H2 2026
catalysts.detail: Each new city is a tangible proof point and the Vegas Cybercab wash site is real physical commitment. But the freshly disclosed 17-crash NHTSA tally raises the headline cost of any safety incident in a new market.

macro_factors.detail: 30-year Treasury yields above 5% raise the discount rate on Tesla's late-decade robotaxi and Optimus cash flows — a direct valuation headwind for AI-premium growth names
The Beijing summit produced no FSD deal and Tesla China's refusal to follow the US price hike signals a colder, more competitive Chinese backdrop where Tesla's NEV share has already shrunk to ~3%

macro_factors.factor: Global EV demand and pricing power
macro_factors.detail: Tesla's first US Model Y price increase in two years is a tentative signal that demand has firmed enough to test pricing power, but Europe deliveries fell ~49% YoY in Q1 and the 50K+ inventory build still hangs over the back half

outlook.summary: The next three to six months hinge on whether Tesla can convert narrative into numbers — and for the first time in six weeks, Wall Street as a whole is sceptical, with the consensus 12-month target ($403.86) sitting below Friday's close. The weekend Model Y price hike is the most important new input since the China summit: it directly addresses the auto-margin question that defines Q2 and, if order rates hold at the higher price, could push consensus targets back above spot. Bears will point to the trailing 416x P/E, 50K+ inventory build, no China FSD, Tesla China refusing to mirror the US price move, and confirmed negative free cash flow for the rest of 2026. Bulls have real proof points to lean on: Optimus V3 lines being installed at Fremont, a Cybercab wash site breaking ground in Vegas, FSD subscriptions growing 51% YoY, and now a credible margin tailwind. The Q2 delivery report in early July is the next hard catalyst; until then expect choppy two-way trading inside a $400-$450 range, with directional breakouts requiring either a China FSD surprise (bull case) or a robotaxi safety incident (bear case).

recommendation.reasoning: The bull-bear balance is now genuinely even, which is exactly what a HOLD looks like. Bullish: Golden Cross is intact, price is comfortably above both the 50-day and 200-day moving averages, RSI is neutral with room to move either way, the fundamental story keeps adding proof points (Optimus V3 lines, Cybercab wash site, FSD subs +51% YoY), and the weekend US Model Y price hike is a fresh, tangible margin catalyst that directly answers the question Q2 will be judged on. Bearish: Friday's -4.75% drop broke the rally structure and pushed price below the 5-day MA, MACD momentum is rolling over, Wall Street's average target ($403.86) still sits below spot, 30-year yields above 5% are a direct discount-rate headwind, and Tesla China refused to follow the US price hike — capping the bull case on the most important international market. Confidence is Medium because either scenario remains live and a single catalyst (China FSD landing, or a robotaxi safety incident) could resolve the picture either way within days. What flips this call: a decisive break above $445 with above-average volume turns this BUY; a weekly close below $416 (the 50-day MA) turns it SELL.
recommendation.key_risk: A failure to defend $416 (the 50-day MA) this week would close the medium-term cushion that has supported the rebound since early May, opening a likely retest of the high-$390s. With Wall Street's consensus target already below spot, there is little analyst-target support to cushion a deeper drawdown — and a follow-on robotaxi safety event in any of the new launch cities could accelerate the downside.
